era_context: The mid-12th century saw the continued fragmentation of the Angevin Empire under Stephen's declining rule, the consolidation of Nur ad-Din's power in the Levant, the height of the Crusader States, and the transition of power in Japan. In China, the Jin dynasty controlled the north while the Southern Song dynasty held the south. The Second Crusade was in its final stages.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Eastern Europe and Rus' principalities (sparse Western sources)
Southeast Asia and the Pacific (limited contemporary records)

events.title: Eleanor of Aquitaine marries Henry of Anjou; union of vast territories
events.description: Eleanor of Aquitaine, recently divorced from King Louis VII of France, married Henry, Duke of Normandy and Count of Anjou, on 18 May 1152. This marriage united the vast territories of Aquitaine (Eleanor's inheritance) with Henry's Norman and Angevin lands, creating an empire that would eventually stretch from Scotland to the Pyrenees. Eleanor brought not only her lands but also her political acumen and cultural influence. This marriage would have profound consequences for the history of England and France, as Henry would become Henry II of England in 1154, and Eleanor would become one of the most influential women of the medieval period.
key_figures: Henry, Duke of Normandy and Anjou (future Henry II of England)

sources.name: William of Newburgh, Historia Rerum Anglicarum

events.certainty_note: The marriage of Eleanor and Henry on 18 May 1152 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: Leads to 1154-001 (Henry II's accession as king)
Leads to 1170-001 (Becket murder, involving Henry II and Eleanor's influence)

events.title: Frederick Barbarossa elected Holy Roman Emperor
events.description: Frederick I, known as Frederick Barbarossa (Red Beard), was elected King of the Romans on 4 March 1152 and crowned Holy Roman Emperor in 1155. Frederick's election marked the beginning of the Hohenstaufen dynasty's dominance in the Holy Roman Empire and initiated a period of imperial expansion and conflict with the papacy. Frederick's reign would be marked by attempts to reassert imperial authority in Italy and conflicts with the Guelphs and other German princes. His election represented a shift toward stronger imperial rule after the weak reign of Conrad III.
key_figures: Frederick I Barbarossa (newly elected King of the Romans)

sources.name: Otto of Freising, Gesta Friderici Imperatoris

events.certainty_note: Frederick Barbarossa's election on 4 March 1152 is well-documented in contemporary German and Italian sources.
cross_references: Leads to 1155-001 (Frederick's coronation as Holy Roman Emperor)
Leads to 1176-001 (Battle of Legnano, Frederick's defeat by the Lombard League)

events.title: Nur ad-Din consolidates control of Aleppo and northern Syria
events.description: Nur ad-Din Mahmud continued his consolidation of power in Syria, solidifying his control over Aleppo and northern Syria. By 1152, he had effectively eliminated most rival Zengid branches and established himself as the dominant Muslim power in the Levant. This consolidation was crucial for the eventual rise of Saladin and the Ayyubid dynasty. Nur ad-Din's rule brought a degree of stability and religious fervor to the region, with a focus on the jihad against the Crusader States.

sources.name: Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of Syria

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Nur ad-Din's power occurred over several years; 1152 marks a conventional point of reference for his established dominance, though exact dating of individual consolidations varies in sources.

events.title: Raynald of Châtillon becomes Prince of Antioch
events.description: Raynald of Châtillon, a Norman adventurer, became Prince of Antioch through his marriage to Constance of Antioch, widow of the previous prince. Raynald's rule was marked by aggressive military campaigns against Muslim territories and a reputation for brutality. His tenure as Prince of Antioch (1152-1160) would be characterized by conflict with Nur ad-Din and the Byzantine Empire. Raynald would later become a major figure in the conflicts leading to the Battle of Hattin in 1187.
events.certainty_note: Raynald's marriage to Constance and his assumption of the principality of Antioch in 1152 is well-documented in Crusader sources.
cross_references: Leads to 1160-002 (Raynald's capture by Nur ad-Din)
Leads to 1187-001 (Battle of Hattin, where Raynald plays a role)

events.title: Suger, Abbot of Saint-Denis, dies; end of an era of monastic patronage
events.description: Suger, the influential Abbot of Saint-Denis and advisor to French kings, died in 1152. Suger had been a major patron of the arts and architecture, and his patronage of the reconstruction of the Basilica of Saint-Denis is credited with initiating the Gothic architectural style. His death marked the end of an era of monastic influence on royal politics and culture in France. Suger's legacy would continue to influence French architecture and royal ideology for centuries.

sources.name: Vita Sugerii (Life of Suger, by various authors)

events.certainty_note: Suger's death in 1152 is well-documented in monastic records and hagiographical sources.
cross_references: Leads to 1154-003 (Al-Idrisi's completion of his geographical work, contemporary cultural achievement)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Raynald of Châtillon's assumption of the principality of Antioch (1152-004) is sometimes overstated in Western historiography; while he was an important figure, his role was not as dominant as some sources suggest, and his principality was increasingly constrained by Nur ad-Din's power. The dating of Suger's death is sometimes given as 1151 in some sources, though 1152 is more commonly accepted.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1152 is moderate, with good documentation from Western European and Islamic sources. The marriage of Eleanor and Henry is particularly well-documented due to its political significance. However, sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events involving the elite. Economic and social history is sparse. The death of Suger is well-documented in monastic sources but may be overrepresented in Western historiography.

graph_edges.note: Eleanor's marriage to Henry enabled his acquisition of vast territories and his subsequent accession as Henry II
Frederick Barbarossa's election led to his coronation as Holy Roman Emperor in 1155
Raynald's assumption of the principality of Antioch led to his capture by Nur ad-Din in 1160
Suger's death and al-Idrisi's completion of his geographical work represent parallel cultural achievements in different regions
